request.question: What is the fourth episode in the American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon titled "Community"?

title: Rick and Morty
HarmonQuest
The Sarah Silverman Program
Harmontown
Great Minds with Dan Harmon
Acceptable.TV
Basic Rocket Science
Community (TV series)
List of Community characters
Introduction to Finality
sentences: Rick and Morty is an American adult animated science-fiction sitcom created by Justin Roiland and Dan Harmon for Cartoon Network's late-night programming block Adult Swim.

The series follows the misadventures of cynical mad scientist Rick Sanchez and his fretful, easily influenced grandson Morty Smith, who split their time between domestic family life and interdimensional adventures.
Roiland voices the series' eponymous characters, with the voice talent of Chris Parnell, Spencer Grammer, and Sarah Chalke providing the rest of the family.
It premiered on December 2, 2013.
HarmonQuest is an American adult animation/live improvisation television show created by Dan Harmon and Spencer Crittenden.
The show is part animated, part live action.
Dan Harmon and comedians Erin McGathy, Jeff B. Davis, along with game master Spencer Crittenden, perform a fantasy roleplaying campaign in front of a live audience at Victory Studios in Glendale, CA.
Each episode of the show also features a special guest player.

The content of their stories is then animated by Starburns Industries, and the episodes feature a mix of live and animated footage reminiscent of "The Ricky Gervais Show", which Harmon cited as an influence during a Facebook Live tour of Starburns Industries.

The Sarah Silverman Program is an American television sitcom, which ran from February 1, 2007 to April 15, 2010 on Comedy Central starring comedian and actress Sarah Silverman, who created the series with Dan Harmon and Rob Schrab.
Harmontown is a weekly live comedy podcast that began airing on June 6, 2012.
It is hosted by writer Dan Harmon, best known as the creator of the sitcom "Community", and actor Jeff B. Davis, best known for his work on the improvisational comedy series "Whose Line Is It Anyway?
" "Harmontown" began as a monthly live comedy show in Los Angeles, California at the NerdMelt Showroom on May 23, 2011, but the show became weekly after Harmon was fired from "Community".
Since the summer of 2012, each episode of "Harmontown" has been recorded and released as a podcast by Feral Audio.
Great Minds with Dan Harmon is an American comedy television series that premiered on February 25, 2016 on the History channel.

The series stars writer Dan Harmon (creator of "Community" and co-creator of "Rick and Morty") and his assistant Spencer Crittenden as themselves, who transport a series of historical figures to the present.
The series is part of History's "Night Class" programming block and was available to stream on History's website and YouTube channel.
The videos have since been removed but short clips are still available on YouTube.
Acceptable.TV is a television program created by Dan Harmon and Rob Schrab that aired on VH1 from March 23 to May 11, 2007.
Each show was composed of several mini-episodes created by the Acceptable.TV staff, with one mini-episode that was submitted by a viewer.
After each episode viewers would be able to vote online for their two favorites.
The two that received the most votes will be continued in the following episode, and the remaining three would be cancelled and replaced by new mini-shows.
The show was adapted from Harmon and Schrab's Channel 101 screenings, and the cast was composed of various Channel 101 contributors.
"Basic Rocket Science" is the fourth episode of the second season of "Community".
It was originally broadcast on October 14, 2010, on NBC.
Community is an American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon that aired on NBC and Yahoo! Screen from September 17, 2009 to June 2, 2015.

The series follows an ensemble cast of characters played by Joel McHale, Gillian Jacobs, Danny Pudi, Yvette Nicole Brown, Alison Brie, Donald Glover, Ken Jeong, Chevy Chase, and Jim Rash at a community college in the fictional town of Greendale, Colorado.
It makes heavy use of meta-humor and pop culture references, often parodying film and television clichés and tropes.
Community is an American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon.
The show, set at the fictional Greendale Community College, depicts the on-campus exploits of students brought together when they form a study group.
The show stars:
"Introduction to Finality" is the 22nd episode of the third season of the American television series "Community" and the third season finale.
It originally aired on May 17, 2012 on NBC.
This was the last episode to air with series creator Dan Harmon as showrunner before he was fired, though Harmon would later return as showrunner for the 5th season.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: What is the fourth episode in the American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon titled "Community"?
hop1_docs: List of Community characters | Community is an American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon.  The show, set at the fictional Greendale Community College, depicts the on-campus exploits of students brought together when they form a study group.  The show stars:

Emotional Consequences of Broadcast Television | "Emotional Consequences of Broadcast Television" is the series finale of the sitcom "Community", serving as the thirteenth episode of its sixth season.  It was written by series creator Dan Harmon and Chris McKenna, and directed by Rob Schrab.  It is the 110th episode overall and was first released on Yahoo! Screen in the United States on June 2, 2015.

Dan Harmon | Daniel Harmon (born January 3, 1973) is an American writer, producer, and voice actor.  Harmon created and produced the NBC comedy television series "Community", co-created the Adult Swim animated series "Rick and Morty", and co-founded the alternative television network/website Channel 101.  Harmon published "You'll Be Perfect When You're Dead" in 2013.  He also hosts a weekly eponymous podcast, "Harmontown."

Community (TV series) | Community is an American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon that aired on NBC and Yahoo! Screen from September 17, 2009 to June 2, 2015.  The series follows an ensemble cast of characters played by Joel McHale, Gillian Jacobs, Danny Pudi, Yvette Nicole Brown, Alison Brie, Donald Glover, Ken Jeong, Chevy Chase, and Jim Rash at a community college in the fictional town of Greendale, Colorado.  It makes heavy use of meta-humor and pop culture references, often parodying film and television clichés and tropes.

Introduction to Finality | "Introduction to Finality" is the 22nd episode of the third season of the American television series "Community" and the third season finale.  It originally aired on May 17, 2012 on NBC.  This was the last episode to air with series creator Dan Harmon as showrunner before he was fired, though Harmon would later return as showrunner for the 5th season.

The Sarah Silverman Program | The Sarah Silverman Program is an American television sitcom, which ran from February 1, 2007 to April 15, 2010 on Comedy Central starring comedian and actress Sarah Silverman, who created the series with Dan Harmon and Rob Schrab.

HarmonQuest | HarmonQuest is an American adult animation/live improvisation television show created by Dan Harmon and Spencer Crittenden.  The show is part animated, part live action.  Dan Harmon and comedians Erin McGathy, Jeff B. Davis, along with game master Spencer Crittenden, perform a fantasy roleplaying campaign in front of a live audience at Victory Studios in Glendale, CA.  Each episode of the show also features a special guest player.  The content of their stories is then animated by Starburns Industries, and the episodes feature a mix of live and animated footage reminiscent of "The Ricky Gervais Show", which Harmon cited as an influence during a Facebook Live tour of Starburns Industries.
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found:
  - Dan Harmon: passage 3 ("Dan Harmon | Daniel Harmon (born January 3, 1973) ... Harmon created and produced the NBC comedy television series 'Community'") — cite: passage 3 ("Dan Harmon").
  - Community (the series): passage 4 ("Community is an American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon") — cite: passage 4 ("Community (TV series)").
  - Example episode pages present in the passages (show that episode pages exist, but not the 4th): passage 2 quotes "'Emotional Consequences of Broadcast Television' ... 110th episode overall" — cite: passage 2 ("Emotional Consequences of Broadcast Television"); passage 5 quotes "'Introduction to Finality' is the 22nd episode of the third season" — cite: passage 5 ("Introduction to Finality").
- Ambiguities:
  - The passages do not include a "List of Community episodes" or a Season 1 episode list page. It is therefore unclear from these passages which page holds the "fourth episode" title.
  - "Fourth episode" could mean (a) the fourth episode overall (series-wide), or (b) season 1 episode 4. The passages do not disambiguate which interpretation is intended.
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Bridge entity (as it appears in the passages): "Community (TV series)".
  - Precise missing fact needed: the exact title of the fourth episode (specify whether overall episode #4 or Season 1, Episode 4).
- Retrieval direction (prioritized):
  1) Open "List of Community episodes" (or "Community episodes" / "List of Community episodes (TV series)") — check the Episodes list/table and look for overall episode #4 and Season 1, Episode 4 rows (lede/episode table). Example search: "List of Community episodes Wikipedia" or "Community episode list".
  2) Open "Community (season 1)" or the "Season 1 (2009–10) episodes" section on the "Community (TV series)" page — check the Season 1 episode table and the row for episode 4 (check title, episode number, and air date). Example search: "Community season 1 episode list" or "Community season 1 episodes Episode 4".
  3) If those pages are absent or ambiguous, fetch the specific episode pages around early season 1 (e.g., pages for episodes titled in season 1) or the series' "Episodes" section on the "Community (TV series)" page.
Final answer deferred until those pages/sections are retrieved.
hop2_docs: List of virtual communities | This is a list of Wikipedia articles about virtual communities.

The Signpost | The Signpost (formerly The Wikipedia Signpost) is the English Wikipedia's online newspaper.  Managed by the Wikipedia community, it is published online with contributions from Wikipedia editors.  The newspaper reports on the Wikipedia community and events related to Wikipedia, including Arbitration Committee rulings, Wikimedia Foundation issues, and other Wikipedia-related projects.

Wikipedia community | The Wikipedia community is the community of contributors to the online encyclopedia Wikipedia.  Individual contributors are known as "Wikipedians". " OxfordDictionaries.com" added the word "Wikipedian" in August 2012.

Wiki-PR editing of Wikipedia | Wiki-PR is a consulting firm that formerly marketed the ability to edit Wikipedia by "...directly edit[ing] your page using our network of established Wikipedia editors and admin[s]."  It was then banned, including all of its employees, contractors, and owners, by the Wikipedia community for unethical editing.  Despite the ban, the firm still markets (as of 2017 ) its ability to advise clients on how to interact with the Wikipedia community.
List of Wikipedias | This is the list of the different language editions of Wikipedia; as of 2017 there are 299 Wikipedias of which 288 are active and 11 are not.

Wikipedia – A New Community of Practice? | Wikipedia: A New Community of Practice?  is a 2009 book by British historian Dan O'Sullivan.  The book takes an academic approach to Wikipedia, applying the ideas of theorists like Jürgen Habermas, Michael Warner, and Roland Barthes.

Criticism of Wikipedia | Criticism of Wikipedia—of its content, procedures, and operations, and of the Wikipedia community—covers many subjects, topics, and themes about the nature of Wikipedia as an open source encyclopedia of subject entries that almost anyone can edit.  Wikipedia has been criticized for the uneven handling, acceptance, and retention of articles about controversial subjects.  The principal concerns of its critics are the factual reliability of the content; the readability of the prose; and a clear article layout; the existence of systemic bias; of gender bias; and of racial bias among the editorial community that is Wikipedia.  Further concerns are that the organization allows the participation of anonymous editors (leading to editorial vandalism); the existence of social stratification (allowing cliques); and over-complicated rules (allowing editorial quarrels), the conditions of which permit the misuse of Wikipedia.
hop2_titles: Wikipedia – A New Community of Practice?
response.summary_2: The passages do not specify the title of the fourth episode of Community. Community is an American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon.
response.answer: Not specified in the provided summaries.
